context: Mostly private cemetery markers and small battlefield memorials erected by Confederate veterans' groups
Slow growth as the UCV (United Confederate Veterans) begins organizing
events: Federal troops withdrawn from the South (1877)

context: The United Daughters of the Confederacy founded in 1894. Monument construction accelerates as Jim Crow laws begin spreading across the South.
Jim Crow laws now law across the South. The UDC launches organized campaigns to erect monuments at courthouses and public squares — deliberately in spaces of civic power, not cemeteries.
events: Plessy v. Ferguson — separate but equal (1896)

context: Peak of the first Jim Crow wave. The NAACP is founded in direct response to escalating racial terror. Confederate monuments are being placed at courthouses — the centers of legal power over Black citizens.
Birth of a Nation released (1915) sparks KKK revival — membership grows from ~5,000 to nearly 5 million. Monument construction remains at historic highs.
events: KKK revival — membership peaks at ~5 million (1924)

context: First wave slows as KKK influence recedes. Many monuments now established in place.
Depression and wartime reduce monument construction significantly.
events: Gone With the Wind released (1939) — 8 Oscars, seen by more Americans than any prior film

context: Post-war period sees modest uptick as Southern identity politics re-emerge. Dixiecrat walkout at 1948 Democratic convention.

The largest single-decade spike in the entire dataset. Monument construction surges in direct response to the Civil Rights movement. Schools, highways, and public buildings are named for Confederate figures as a message to Black citizens and the federal government.
events: Little Rock Central High School crisis (1957)

context: Voting Rights Act passes. Construction declines but continues as backlash to federal enforcement.
Monument construction drops to historic lows as the Civil Rights era fades from direct political conflict.
Small uptick tied to Confederate flag and heritage debates in the South.
events: Confederate flag controversy intensifies across Southern state capitals

context: Modest construction continues. Growing counter-movement begins documenting and challenging monuments.
Charlottesville (2017) accelerates removals that began after the Charleston church massacre. Over 100 monuments removed 2017–2021.
events: George Floyd murder / nationwide removal wave (2020)
